original_hypothesis.title: PEMCertChainLib _findTcb Array Out-of-Bounds When PCESVN Is Not Last SVN Entry
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _findTcb, cpusvns array has size SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE (16) but the loop iterates SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1 (17) times. When the PCESVN_OID is encountered, the value is stored in pcesvn (not cpusvns). If PCESVN_OID is NOT the last entry in the DER sequence, the 17th iteration (i=16) would attempt cpusvns[16], causing an out-of-bounds revert in Solidity 0.8.x. Additionally, if PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns has a zero-valued gap at that index.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code assumes PCESVN_OID is always the last SVN component. If Intel changes the PCK certificate format or the SVN ordering changes, all attestation verification would permanently revert. The zero-gap issue could also cause incorrect TCB level matching.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cpusvns = new uint256[](16); loop 0..16 (17 iterations); cpusvns[i] written when i=16 and not PCESVN causes OOB

preconditions: PCK certificate has PCESVN_OID not as the last SVN entry in the DER sequence
Or an extra non-PCESVN SVN component exists at position 16

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Array sized 16 with loop iterating 17 times; out-of-bounds write if PCESVN not last
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cpusvns[i] = cpusvn where i can be 16 (array size 16, valid indices 0-15)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check on i before array write; no explicit handling of PCESVN position

analysis_questions: What is the actual ordering of SVN components in Intel PCK certificates?
Is PCESVN always the last component in current Intel SGX certificate implementations?
Does the zero-gap in cpusvns affect TCB level comparison results?
validation_requirements: Parse a real Intel PCK certificate and verify SVN ordering
Test with PCESVN_OID at non-final position to trigger OOB
Verify cpusvns values with PCESVN at different positions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Intel changes PCK certificate format such that PCESVN is not the last SVN entry, _findTcb would attempt cpusvns[16] (OOB), causing all verifyAttestation calls to revert. This would constitute a permanent DoS of the attestation system.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of attestation verification if triggered
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on SGX attestation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract redeployment with fixed parsing

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-standard SVN ordering in PCK certificates

validation_result.impact_summary: Permanent DoS of attestation verification if Intel changes PCK certificate SVN ordering.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `_findTcb` function in `PEMCertChainLib.sol` parses the TCB sequence from an Intel SGX PCK certificate. It initializes the `cpusvns` array with a length of 16 (`SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE`). The loop then iterates 17 times (`SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1`) to extract 16 CPUSVN values and 1 PCESVN value. Inside the loop, the code checks if the current OID matches `PCESVN_OID`. If it does, it assigns the value to `pcesvn`. Otherwise, it assigns the value to `cpusvns[i]`. Because the loop index `i` is used directly to index the `cpusvns` array, the code implicitly assumes that `PCESVN_OID` will always be the very last element (at `i = 16`). If a valid certificate is processed where `PCESVN_OID` is located at any index other than 16, two issues occur: 1. At the index where `PCESVN_OID` is found, `cpusvns[i]` is skipped, leaving a zero value in the array. 2. At `i = 16`, a CPUSVN value will be processed, and the code will attempt to execute `cpusvns[16] = cpusvn`. Since the array length is 16, this results in an out-of-bounds array access, causing the transaction to revert. This makes the attestation verification extremely fragile to any changes in Intel's certificate generation process.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code snippet in `PEMCertChainLib.sol` lines 246-265 clearly shows the array initialization `cpusvns = new uint256[](SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE);` and the loop `for (uint256 i; i < SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1; ++i)`. The assignment `cpusvns[i] = cpusvn;` inside the `else` block will revert if `i == 16`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The loop iterates 17 times, but `cpusvns` has a size of 16. If `PCESVN_OID` is not at `i == 16`, the `else` block will execute when `i == 16`, attempting to write to `cpusvns[16]`, which causes an out-of-bounds revert.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `verifyAttestation` is called, it parses the quote and decodes the PCK certificate using `PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert`. This function extracts the SGX extensions and calls `_findPckTcbInfo`, which in turn calls `_findTcb` to parse the SVN values. In `_findTcb`, the loop iterates 17 times over the SVN sequence. If the `PCESVN_OID` is not the last element, the 17th iteration will process a CPUSVN and attempt to write to `cpusvns[16]`, causing an out-of-bounds revert.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker cannot forge a certificate to trigger this maliciously because the certificate must be signed by Intel. However, this is a severe reliability and compatibility defect. If Intel updates their certificate generation such that the SVN sequence order changes (e.g., placing PCESVN first), the entire attestation system will permanently break, resulting in a Denial of Service for all protocol users relying on SGX attestation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Permanent DoS of attestation verification if triggered by a change in Intel's certificate format. Requires contract redeployment to fix.
recommended_mitigations: Use a separate index variable for the `cpusvns` array that only increments when a CPUSVN is added, rather than relying on the loop index `i`.
Example: `uint256 cpuIdx = 0;` before the loop, and `cpusvns[cpuIdx++] = uint256(svnValue);` inside the `else` block, along with a bounds check `require(cpuIdx <= SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE)`.
validation_result.replication_plan: Create a mock PCK certificate DER encoding where the PCESVN OID is placed before the 16th CPUSVN OID. Pass this DER encoding to `PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert` and observe the transaction revert with an out-of-bounds error.
uncertainties: Whether Intel has any plans or existing certificates that deviate from the standard SVN ordering.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz testing of `PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert` with validly structured but reordered ASN.1 sequences to ensure no other parsing assumptions cause reverts.
